</tr><tr><td class="sidebar-navbar"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333133064"><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1239400231">.mw-parser-output .navbar{display:inline;font-size:88%;font-weight:normal}.mw-parser-output .navbar-collapse{float:left;text-align:left}.mw-parser-output .navbar-boxtext{word-spacing:0}.mw-parser-output .navbar ul{display:inline-block;white-space:nowrap;line-height:inherit}.mw-parser-output .navbar-brackets::before{margin-right:-0.125em;content:"[ "}.mw-parser-output .navbar-brackets::after{margin-left:-0.125em;content:" ]"}.mw-parser-output .navbar li{word-spacing:-0.125em}.mw-parser-output .navbar a>span,.mw-parser-output .navbar a>abbr{text-decoration:inherit}.mw-parser-output .navbar-mini abbr{font-variant:small-caps;border-bottom:none;text-decoration:none;cursor:inherit}.mw-parser-output .navbar-ct-full{font-size:114%;margin:0 7em}.mw-parser-output .navbar-ct-mini{font-size:114%;margin:0 4em}html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .navbar li a abbr{color:var(--color-base)!important}@media(prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .navbar li a abbr{color:var(--color-base)!important}}@media print{.mw-parser-output .navbar{display:none!important}}</style><div class="navbar plainlinks hlist navbar-mini"><ul><li class="nv-view"><a href="/wiki/Template:Ancient_Roman_religion_sidebar" title="Template:Ancient Roman religion sidebar"><abbr title="View this template">v</abbr></a></li><li class="nv-talk"><a href="/wiki/Template_talk:Ancient_Roman_religion_sidebar" title="Template talk:Ancient Roman religion sidebar"><abbr title="Discuss this template">t</abbr></a></li><li class="nv-edit"><a href="/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Ancient_Roman_religion_sidebar" title="Special:EditPage/Template:Ancient Roman religion sidebar"><abbr title="Edit this template">e</abbr></a></li></ul></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Minerva-Vedder-Highsmith-detail-1.jpeg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Minerva-Vedder-Highsmith-detail-1.jpeg/250px-Minerva-Vedder-Highsmith-detail-1.jpeg" decoding="async" width="190" height="301" style="--mw-file-upright: 0.75" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Minerva-Vedder-Highsmith-detail-1.jpeg/500px-Minerva-Vedder-Highsmith-detail-1.jpeg 2x" data-file-width="569" data-file-height="901"></a><figcaption>Mosaic of the <i>Minerva of Peace</i> in the <a href="/wiki/Library_of_Congress" title="Library of Congress">Library of Congress</a></figcaption></figure>
<p><b>Minerva</b> (<span class="rt-commentedText nowrap"><span class="IPA nopopups noexcerpt" lang="en-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Help:IPA/English" title="Help:IPA/English">/<span style="border-bottom:1px dotted"><span title="'m' in 'my'">m</span><span title="/ɪ/: 'i' in 'kit'">ɪ</span><span title="/ˈ/: primary stress follows">ˈ</span><span title="'n' in 'nigh'">n</span><span title="/ɜːr/: 'ur' in 'fur'">ɜːr</span><span title="'v' in 'vie'">v</span><span title="/ə/: 'a' in 'about'">ə</span></span>/</a></span></span>; <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1177148991">.mw-parser-output .IPA-label-small{font-size:85%}.mw-parser-output .references .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .infobox .IPA-label-small,.mw-parser-output .navbox .IPA-label-small{font-size:100%}</style><span class="IPA-label IPA-label-small">Latin:</span> <span class="IPA nowrap" lang="la-Latn-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin" title="Help:IPA/Latin">[mɪˈnɛru̯ä]</a></span>; <a href="/wiki/Etruscan_language" title="Etruscan language">Etruscan</a>: <i lang="ett">Menrva</i>) is the <a href="/wiki/Roman_mythology" title="Roman mythology">Roman goddess</a> of <a href="/wiki/Wisdom" title="Wisdom">wisdom</a>, justice, law, victory, and the sponsor of arts, trade, and strategy. She is also a goddess of warfare, though with a focus on strategic warfare, rather than the violence of gods such as <a href="/wiki/Mars_(mythology)" title="Mars (mythology)">Mars</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Bulfinch-2010_1-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bulfinch-2010-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Beginning in the second century BC, the <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Rome" title="Ancient Rome">Romans</a> <a href="/wiki/Religious_syncretism" title="Religious syncretism">equated</a> her with the Greek <a href="/wiki/Goddess" title="Goddess">goddess</a> <a href="/wiki/Athena" title="Athena">Athena</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Lar2_2-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lar2-2"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Minerva is one of the three Roman deities in the <a href="/wiki/Capitoline_Triad" title="Capitoline Triad">Capitoline Triad</a>, along with <a href="/wiki/Jupiter_(mythology)" class="mw-redirect" title="Jupiter (mythology)">Jupiter</a> and <a href="/wiki/Juno_(mythology)" title="Juno (mythology)">Juno</a>.
</p><p>Minerva is a <a href="/wiki/Virginity" title="Virginity">virgin</a> goddess. Her domain includes <a href="/wiki/Medicine" title="Medicine">medicine</a>, <a href="/wiki/Wisdom" title="Wisdom">wisdom</a>, <a href="/wiki/Commerce" title="Commerce">commerce</a>, <a href="/wiki/Weaving" title="Weaving">weaving</a>, and the <a href="/wiki/Craft" title="Craft">crafts</a>. Minerva is often depicted with her sacred creature, an owl usually named the "<a href="/wiki/Owl_of_Minerva" class="mw-redirect" title="Owl of Minerva">owl of Minerva</a>",<sup id="cite_ref-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-3"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> which symbolised her association with wisdom and knowledge, as well as, less frequently, the snake and the <a href="/wiki/Olive_tree" class="mw-redirect" title="Olive tree">olive tree</a>. Minerva is commonly depicted as tall with an athletic and muscular build. She is often wearing armour and carrying a spear. As an important Roman goddess, she is highly revered, honored, and respected.<sup id="cite_ref-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-4"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Marcus_Terentius_Varro" title="Marcus Terentius Varro">Marcus Terentius Varro</a> considered her to be ideal and the plan for the universe personified.<sup id="cite_ref-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-5"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc">
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Etymology">Etymology</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=1" title="Edit section: Etymology"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The name <i>Minerva</i> possibly derives from <a href="/wiki/Proto-Italic_language" title="Proto-Italic language">Proto-Italic</a> <span title="Proto-Italic-language text">*<i lang="itc"><a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Italic/Menezw%C4%81" class="extiw" title="wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Italic/Menezwā">menezwo-</a></i></span>, and perhaps ultimately from <a href="/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_language" title="Proto-Indo-European language">Proto-Indo-European</a> (PIE) <span title="Proto-Indo-European-language text">*<i lang="ine"><a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/m%C3%A9nos" class="extiw" title="wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/ménos">ménos</a></i></span> ("thought"). <a href="/wiki/Helmut_Rix" title="Helmut Rix">Helmut Rix</a> (1981) and Gerhard Meiser (1998) have proposed the PIE derivative <span title="uncoded-language text">*<i lang="mis">menes-weh₂</i></span> ("provided with a mind, intelligent") as the transitional form.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEde_Vaan2008380–381_6-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Vaan2008380–381-6"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, the linguist <a href="/wiki/Benjamin_W._Fortson_IV" title="Benjamin W. Fortson IV">Benjamin W. Fortson IV</a> criticizes the connection with the root <span title="Proto-Indo-European-language text">*<i lang="ine"><a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/men-" class="extiw" title="wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/men-">men-</a></i></span> ("to think"), arguing that the connections between Minerva and wisdom emerged at a later date under the influence of Athena.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEFortson_IV2017839_7-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFortson_IV2017839-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Another hypothesis, advanced by the linguist <a href="/wiki/Ranko_Matasovi%C4%87" title="Ranko Matasović">Ranko Matasović</a>, connects the term with a possible Proto-Indo-European term <span title="Proto-Indo-European-language text">*<i lang="ine"><a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/m%E1%B8%97h%E2%82%81n%CC%A5s" class="extiw" title="wikt:Reconstruction:Proto-Indo-European/mḗh₁n̥s">méh₁nos-</a></i></span> ("moon"). Alternatively, the name of the goddess may not derive from Indo-European—it may be a borrowing from <a href="/wiki/Etruscan_language" title="Etruscan language">Etruscan</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEMatasović2018_8-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMatasović2018-8"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Origin">Origin</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=2" title="Edit section: Origin"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>According to the <i><a href="/wiki/Encyclopedia_of_Indo-European_Culture" title="Encyclopedia of Indo-European Culture">Encyclopedia of Indo-European Culture</a></i>, Minerva constitutes a type of "transfunctional goddess", a mythic archetype common throughout <a href="/wiki/Indo-European_languages" title="Indo-European languages">Indo-European</a> cultures in which a goddess simultaneously is associated with warfare, a priesthood, and agricultural activity.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEMalloryAdams1997595–596_9-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMalloryAdams1997595–596-9"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The religious role of Minerva can be compared to deities such as <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Iranian_religion" title="Ancient Iranian religion">Iranian</a> <a href="/wiki/Anahita" title="Anahita">Anahaita</a> or <a href="/wiki/Irish_mythology" title="Irish mythology">Irish</a> <a href="/wiki/Macha" title="Macha">Macha</a>, though these goddesses cannot be derived from a singular divinity in the original <a href="/wiki/Proto-Indo-European_mythology" title="Proto-Indo-European mythology">Proto-Indo-European religion</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEMalloryAdams1997595_10-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMalloryAdams1997595-10"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> According to the archaeologist Miriam Dexter, in Indo-European mythologies, war goddesses such as Minerva generally do not take active roles as fighters. Dexter argues that Minerva, though associated with military functions, operates more as a general.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEDexter2006147_11-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDexter2006147-11"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Through her role in the <a href="/wiki/Capitoline_Triad" title="Capitoline Triad">Capitoline Triad</a>, Minerva may distantly reflect the proposed construct of <a href="/wiki/Trifunctional_hypothesis" title="Trifunctional hypothesis">Proto-Indo-European trifunctionality</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEWoodard200639_12-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodard200639-12"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, according to the trifunctional theory, it is likely that Minerva supplanted a preexisting deity, perhaps <a href="/wiki/Quirinus" title="Quirinus">Quirinus</a>, within a hypothetical <a href="/wiki/Archaic_Triad" title="Archaic Triad">Archaic triad</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEWoodard200285_13-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodard200285-13"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The place of Minerva within the Capitoline Triad may also be attested for <a href="/wiki/Falisci" title="Falisci">Faliscan</a> religion, as one inscription from Faliscan emigrants in <a href="/wiki/Sardinia" title="Sardinia">Sardinia</a> dated to the 2nd-century BCE mentions the deities <span title="Faliscan-language text"><i lang="xfa">[di]ouei</i></span>, <span title="Faliscan-language text"><i lang="xfa">iunonei</i></span>, and <span title="Faliscan-language text"><i lang="xfa">mineruai</i></span>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001131_14-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001131-14"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEBakkum2009498–499_15-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBakkum2009498–499-15"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Minerva may have acquired her role in the Capitoline triad under Etruscan influence, as a similar set of Etruscan deities consisting of <a href="/wiki/Tinia" title="Tinia">Tinia</a>, <a href="/wiki/Uni_(mythology)" title="Uni (mythology)">Uni</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Menrva" title="Menrva">Menrva</a> is known to have existed.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEDenova201840_16-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDenova201840-16"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Minerva has been interpreted as a borrowing from Menrva,<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEDenova201878_17-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDenova201878-17"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> though it is also possible that the Etruscan deity was a borrowing from Minerva.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEWoodard200643_18-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodard200643-18"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The Roman 1st-century BCE <a href="/wiki/Marcus_Terentius_Varro" title="Marcus Terentius Varro">Varro</a> author attributes <a href="/wiki/Sabines" title="Sabines">Sabine</a> origins to the goddess, writing "<a href="/wiki/Feronia_(mythology)" title="Feronia (mythology)">Feronia</a>, Minerva, and the <a href="/wiki/Novensiles" title="Novensiles">Novensides</a> are from the Sabines."<sup id="cite_ref-19" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-19"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The name Minerva may also have been uncovered in an Oscan inscription which may contain the term <i><b><span title="Oscan-language text"><span lang="osc"><a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%90%8C%8C%F0%90%8C%84%F0%90%8C%8D%F0%90%8C%84%F0%90%8C%93%F0%90%8C%84(%F0%90%8C%85%F0%90%8C%80%F0%90%8C%94)" class="extiw" title="wikt:𐌌𐌄𐌍𐌄𐌓𐌄(𐌅𐌀𐌔)">menere(vas)</a></span></span></b></i>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEde_Vaan2008380–381_6-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Vaan2008380–381-6"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, the philologist <a href="/wiki/Carl_Darling_Buck" title="Carl Darling Buck">Carl Darling Buck</a> argues that the <a href="/wiki/Rhotacism" title="Rhotacism">rhotacism</a> indicates that the word, and possibly the cult of the goddess itself, was borrowed from another Italic dialect—perhaps Latin. Similarly, the name <span title="Paelignian-language text"><i lang="pgn"><a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/minerua" class="extiw" title="wikt:minerua">minerua</a></i></span> has also been uncovered on a <a href="/wiki/Paeligni" title="Paeligni">Paelignian</a> inscription, though Buck likewise argues that this term was borrowed from another Italic language.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEBuck190421_20-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBuck190421-20"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In contrast, the linguist <a href="/wiki/Michiel_de_Vaan" title="Michiel de Vaan">Michiel de Vaan</a> notes that the rhotacism could reflect a Proto-Italic sound shift of <span title="Proto-Indo-European-language text">*<i lang="ine">-sw-</i></span> to <span title="Proto-Indo-European-language text">*<i lang="ine">-rw-</i></span>, which may also be attested in terms such as <span title="Latin-language text"><i lang="la"><a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/acervus" class="extiw" title="wikt:acervus">acervus</a></i></span>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEde_Vaan2008380–381_6-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Vaan2008380–381-6"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Nevertheless, the purported existence of this sound change in Proto-Italic remains controversial.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEFortson_IV2017839_7-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFortson_IV2017839-7"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Archaeological_excavation" title="Archaeological excavation">Archaeological excavations</a> at a sanctuary in <a href="/wiki/Lavinium" title="Lavinium">Lavinium</a> revealed <a href="/wiki/Terracotta" title="Terracotta">terracotta</a> statues depicting Minerva dating to the 4th-century BCE, indicating the existence of a contemporary cult of the goddess. Other terracotta statuettes from the site varyingly portray unveiled girls with short hair and veiled women with expensive adornments, which likely represent unmarried and married women respectively. Though these votives appear to be natively <a href="/wiki/Italic_peoples" title="Italic peoples">Italic</a>, some of the veiled women are depicted with accompanying <a href="/wiki/Columbidae" title="Columbidae">doves</a>, a design element perhaps attributable to the influence of Greek <a href="/wiki/Aphrodite" title="Aphrodite">Aphrodite</a>. Additional finds from the sanctuary include figurines of <a href="/wiki/Swaddled_infant_votive" title="Swaddled infant votive">swaddled infants</a> and votive depictions of breasts and wombs, all of which reinforce the prominence of childbirth at the site.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001133_21-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001133-21"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> According to the archaeologist Fritz Graf, the evidence from this sanctuary indicates that Minerva operated as a guardian of young women during the transition into marriage and motherhood.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001133_21-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001133-21"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The theme of transition was not, however exclusive to women—votive items from Lavinium portray young men adorned with a <span title="Latin-language text"><i lang="la"><a href="/wiki/Bulla_(amulet)" title="Bulla (amulet)">bulla</a></i></span> offering toys.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001135_22-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001135-22"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The connections between Minerva and childbirth at Lavinium could be construed as a distinction between the Roman divinity and the Greek goddess Athena, who was a virgin deity that actively avoided attempts at procreation, such as in the story of the mythical birth of <a href="/wiki/Erichthonius_(son_of_Hephaestus)" title="Erichthonius (son of Hephaestus)">Erichthonius</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001134_23-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001134-23"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> However, Graf suggests that the childrearing associations may have emerged from the shared characteristic of Athena and Minerva as guardians of young women during transitional phases. In further support of this theory, Graf notes that none of the votives from Lavinium depict conception, arguing that they instead gesture towards potential future childbirth.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001135_22-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001135-22"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> The role of Minerva as a protector of young women may have also contributed to her association with healing,<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001139_24-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001139-24"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> as attested at the Temple of Minerva Medica on the <a href="/wiki/Esquiline_Hill" title="Esquiline Hill">Esquiline Hill</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEBelayche2007284_25-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBelayche2007284-25"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> which contains a deposit of anatomical votives that also dates to the 4th-century BCE.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001138_26-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001138-26"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Alternatively, her medicinal connotations may have emerged from her role as a goddess of crafts, or perhaps due to the influence of <a href="/wiki/Hygieia" title="Hygieia">Athena Hygieia</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001139_24-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001139-24"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Though, a Greek origin for this aspect of Minerva is slightly problematic, as the connection between Athena and Hygieia was largely restricted to <a href="/wiki/Athens" title="Athens">Athens</a> and the Greek divinity functioned as a protector of preexisting health whereas Minerva served to actively treat illness.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001139_24-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001139-24"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Minerva_Mainz.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Minerva_Mainz.jpg/120px-Minerva_Mainz.jpg" decoding="async" width="115" height="264" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Minerva_Mainz.jpg/250px-Minerva_Mainz.jpg 2x" data-file-width="521" data-file-height="1198"></a><figcaption>Minerva with an Owl, <a href="/wiki/Mainz" title="Mainz">Mainz</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>Numerous <a href="/wiki/Warp-weighted_loom" title="Warp-weighted loom">loom weights</a> have also been discovered at Lavinium, possibly in relation to the traditional association of women in prehistoric Italy with <a href="/wiki/Weaving" title="Weaving">weaving</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001133_21-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001133-21"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Alternatively, these items could be related to the function of Athena in her role as <span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language text"><i lang="grc">Ergane</i></span> <i>("<span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language text"><span lang="grc">ἐργάνη</span></span></i>," "worker"), in which she operated as a patroness of artisanship. In particular, the Greek goddess was associated with weaving at practices such as the <a href="/wiki/Arrhephoria" title="Arrhephoria">Arrhephoria</a>—a ritual involving the weaving of the <a href="/wiki/Peplos" title="Peplos">peplos</a> of Athena. However, the Greek ceremonies were largely reserved for a small sample of young girls, whereas the sanctuary at Lavinium was likely open to a broad swathe of the population.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001134_23-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001134-23"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Regardless, the connection between Athena and craftsmanship could have spurred the acquisition of similar traits for Minerva, whose sacred festival—the <span title="Latin-language text"><i lang="la"><a href="/wiki/Quinquatria" title="Quinquatria">Quinquatria</a></i></span>—was itself associated with artisanry.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001137_27-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001137-27"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Uniquely, in Rome, at least by the late <a href="/wiki/Roman_Republic" title="Roman Republic">Roman Republic</a>, the connection with craftsmanship had arisen as one of the primary characteristics of Minerva, perhaps—according to Graf—because her role as a civic goddess was already fulfilled by Jupiter. In contrast, within Greece, Athena was largely defined by her relationship with the <span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language text"><i lang="grc"><a href="/wiki/Polis" title="Polis">polis</a></i></span>, whereas her artisanship associations were relatively minor.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001138_26-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001138-26"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>It is possible that the association of Minerva with the city center may have been borrowed from Athena in her capacity as <i>Polias</i> (<i><span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language text"><span lang="grc">Πολιάς</span></span></i>, "of the city"), which referred to her role as a protectress of the city.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001131_14-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001131-14"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In <a href="/wiki/Pompeii" title="Pompeii">Pompeii</a>, a votive deposit uncovered near the <a href="/w/index.php?title=Porta_Stabia&action=edit&redlink=1" class="new" title="Porta Stabia (page does not exist)">Porta Stabia</a> contained an ancient terracotta bust that may depict Minerva. It is possible that this statue was intended to sanctify the fortifications, thereby incurring the added support of the divine to safeguard the city.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEvan_der_GraaffEllis2017287_28-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_GraaffEllis2017287-28"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> This particular invocation of Minerva may also reflect the influence of poliad Athena.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEvan_der_GraaffEllis2017288_29-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_GraaffEllis2017288-29"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Livy" title="Livy">Livy</a>, a 1st-century BCE Roman historian, describes a <a href="/wiki/Lectisternium" title="Lectisternium">lectisternium</a> during which a couch ("<span title="Latin-language text"><i lang="la"><a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/pulvinar" class="extiw" title="wikt:pulvinar">pulvīnāria</a></i></span>") was on display for Minerva and <a href="/wiki/Neptune_(mythology)" title="Neptune (mythology)">Neptune</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-30" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-30"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> It is likely that the connection between these two deities also stems from Greek influence, as it mirrors the relation between Athena and <a href="/wiki/Poseidon" title="Poseidon">Poseidon</a> in the founding myth of Athens.<sup id="cite_ref-FOOTNOTEGraf2001131–132_31-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraf2001131–132-31"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Presence_in_mythology">Presence in mythology</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=3" title="Edit section: Presence in mythology"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Minerva is a prominent figure in <a href="/wiki/Roman_mythology" title="Roman mythology">Roman mythology</a>. She appears in many famous myths. Many of the stories of her Greek counterpart <a href="/wiki/Athena" title="Athena">Athena</a> are attributed to Minerva in Roman mythology, such as that of the naming of Athens<sup id="cite_ref-Hyginus-2007_32-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hyginus-2007-32"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> resulting from a competition between Minerva and <a href="/wiki/Neptune_(mythology)" title="Neptune (mythology)">Neptune</a>,<sup id="cite_ref-Ovid-2018_33-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ovid-2018-33"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> in which Minerva created the olive tree.<sup id="cite_ref-Bulfinch-2010_1-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bulfinch-2010-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Birth">Birth</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=4" title="Edit section: Birth"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>The myth of Minerva's birth follows that of Athena. Jupiter raped the <a href="/wiki/Titan_(mythology)" class="mw-redirect" title="Titan (mythology)">titaness</a> Metis, which resulted in her attempting to change shape (or <a href="/wiki/Shapeshift" class="mw-redirect" title="Shapeshift">shapeshift</a>) to escape him. Jupiter then recalled the prophecy that his own child would overthrow him, just as he himself had overthrown his father <a href="/wiki/Saturn_(mythology)" title="Saturn (mythology)">Saturn</a>, and in turn, Saturn had overthrown his father <a href="/wiki/Caelus" title="Caelus">Caelus</a>. Fearing that their child would be male, and would grow stronger than he was and rule the Heavens in his place, Jupiter swallowed Metis whole after tricking her into turning herself into a fly. The Titaness gave birth to Minerva and forged weapons and armour for her child while within Jupiter's body. The constant pounding and ringing left Jupiter with agonizing pain. To relieve the pain, <a href="/wiki/Vulcan_(mythology)" title="Vulcan (mythology)">Vulcan</a> used a hammer to split Jupiter's head and, from the cleft, Minerva emerged, as a grown adult and in full battle armour.<sup class="noprint Inline-Template Template-Fact" style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed" title="Wikipedia:Citation needed"><span title="This claim needs references to reliable sources. (November 2025)">citation needed</span></a></i>]</sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Minerva_and_Arachne">Minerva and Arachne</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=5" title="Edit section: Minerva and Arachne"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Arachne" title="Arachne">Arachne</a> was a mortal highly proficient in weaving and embroidery. Not only were her finished works beautiful, but also her process, so much so that <a href="/wiki/Nymphs" class="mw-redirect" title="Nymphs">nymphs</a> would come out of their natural environments to watch her work. Arachne boasted that her skills could beat those of Minerva, and if she were wrong she would pay the price for it. This angered Minerva, and she took the form of an old woman to approach Arachne, offering her a chance to take back her challenge and ask forgiveness.<sup id="cite_ref-Ovid-2018_33-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ovid-2018-33"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> When Arachne refused, Minerva rid herself of her disguise and took Arachne up on her challenge. Arachne began to weave a tapestry that showed the shortcomings of the gods, while Minerva depicted her competition with Neptune and the gods looking down with disgust on mortals who would dare to challenge them.<sup id="cite_ref-Ovid-2018_33-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ovid-2018-33"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Minerva's weaving was meant as a final warning to her foe to back down. Minerva was insulted by the scenes that Arachne was weaving, and destroyed it. She then touched Arachne on the forehead, which made her feel shame for what she had done, leading her to hang herself. Minerva then felt bad for the woman, and brought her back to life. However, Minerva transformed her into a spider as punishment for her actions, and hanging from a web would forever be a reminder to Arachne of her actions that offended the gods. This story also acted as a warning to mortals not to challenge the gods.<sup id="cite_ref-Bulfinch-2010_1-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bulfinch-2010-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Minerva_and_Medusa">Minerva and Medusa</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=6" title="Edit section: Minerva and Medusa"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Medusa" title="Medusa">Medusa</a> was once a beautiful human girl with magnificent hair, she was also a priestess of Minerva. Neptune was obsessed with her and lusted after her. One day Neptune was watching her pray in the Temple of Minerva and decided to act upon his lustful obsession. He forced himself on Medusa but she was not strong enough to fight him off. Minerva was furious this took place in her temple and she turned Medusa into a monster, replacing her hair with hissing snakes and removing her charm. Medusa turned any living creature she looked upon into stone. Neptune was not ever confronted for his wrongdoings to Medusa. When <a href="/wiki/Perseus" title="Perseus">Perseus</a> approached Medusa he used her reflection in his shield to avoid contact with her eyes, and then beheaded her. Medusa's spilt blood gave birth to Pegasus; which Minerva immediately tamed and gifted to <a href="/wiki/Bellerophon" title="Bellerophon">Bellerophon</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Hyginus-2007_32-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hyginus-2007-32"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> He delivered the severed head to Minerva, who placed its image on her <a href="/wiki/Aegis" title="Aegis">Aegis</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Bulfinch-2010_1-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bulfinch-2010-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Taming_of_Pegasus">Taming of Pegasus</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=7" title="Edit section: Taming of Pegasus"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>When <a href="/wiki/Perseus" title="Perseus">Perseus</a> beheaded <a href="/wiki/Medusa" title="Medusa">Medusa</a> some of the blood spilled onto the ground, and from it came <a href="/wiki/Pegasus" title="Pegasus">Pegasus</a>. Minerva caught the horse and tamed it before gifting the horse to the <a href="/wiki/Muses" title="Muses">Muses</a>. It was a kick from the hoof of Pegasus that opened the fountain <a href="/wiki/Hippocrene" title="Hippocrene">Hippocrene</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Ovid-2018_33-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ovid-2018-33"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> When <a href="/wiki/Bellerophon" title="Bellerophon">Bellerophon</a> later went to fight the <a href="/wiki/Chimera_(mythology)" title="Chimera (mythology)">Chimera</a> he sought to use Pegasus in the fight. In order to do this he slept in Minerva's temple, and she came to him with a golden bridle. When Pegasus saw Bellerophon with the bridle the horse immediately allowed Bellerophon to mount, and they defeated the Chimera.<sup id="cite_ref-Bulfinch-2010_1-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bulfinch-2010-1"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Johann_K%C3%B6nig_-_Minerva_Visiting_the_Muses_on_Mount_Helicon.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Johann_K%C3%B6nig_-_Minerva_Visiting_the_Muses_on_Mount_Helicon.jpg/250px-Johann_K%C3%B6nig_-_Minerva_Visiting_the_Muses_on_Mount_Helicon.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="168" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Johann_K%C3%B6nig_-_Minerva_Visiting_the_Muses_on_Mount_Helicon.jpg/500px-Johann_K%C3%B6nig_-_Minerva_Visiting_the_Muses_on_Mount_Helicon.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2000" data-file-height="1343"></a><figcaption>Painting of Minerva visiting the Muses</figcaption></figure>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Turning_Aglauros_to_stone">Turning Aglauros to stone</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=8" title="Edit section: Turning Aglauros to stone"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Metamorphoses" title="Metamorphoses">Metamorphoses</a> by <a href="/wiki/Ovid" title="Ovid">Ovid</a> tell the story of Minerva and <a href="/wiki/Aglaurus,_daughter_of_Cecrops" title="Aglaurus, daughter of Cecrops">Aglauros</a>. When <a href="/wiki/Mercury_(mythology)" title="Mercury (mythology)">Mercury</a> comes to seduce mortal virgin <a href="/wiki/Herse_of_Athens" class="mw-redirect" title="Herse of Athens">Herse</a>, her sister Aglauros is driven by her greed to help him. Minerva discovers this and is furious with Aglauros. She seeks the assistance of <a href="/wiki/Invidia" title="Invidia">Envy</a>, who fills Aglauros with so much envy for the good fortune of others that she <a href="/wiki/Petrifaction_in_mythology_and_fiction" title="Petrifaction in mythology and fiction">turns to stone</a>. Mercury fails to seduce Herse.<sup id="cite_ref-Ovid-2018_33-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ovid-2018-33"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Minerva_and_Hercules">Minerva and Hercules</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=9" title="Edit section: Minerva and Hercules"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Minerva assisted the hero <a href="/wiki/Hercules" title="Hercules">Hercules</a>. In <a href="/wiki/Gaius_Julius_Hyginus" title="Gaius Julius Hyginus">Hyginus</a>' <i><a href="/wiki/Fabulae" title="Fabulae">Fabulae</a></i> she is said to have helped him kill the <a href="/wiki/Lernaean_Hydra" title="Lernaean Hydra">Hydra</a> (30.3).<sup id="cite_ref-Hyginus-2007_32-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hyginus-2007-32"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Minerva_and_Ulysses">Minerva and Ulysses</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=10" title="Edit section: Minerva and Ulysses"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Minerva assisted the hero <a href="/wiki/Odysseus" title="Odysseus">Ulysses</a>. Hyginus describes in his work <i><a href="/wiki/Fabulae" title="Fabulae">Fabulae</a></i> that Minerva changes Odysseus' appearance in order to protect and assist him multiple times (126).<sup id="cite_ref-Hyginus-2007_32-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hyginus-2007-32"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Inventing_the_flute">Inventing the flute</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=11" title="Edit section: Inventing the flute"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Minerva is thought to have invented the <a href="/wiki/Flute" title="Flute">flute</a> by piercing holes into boxwood. She enjoyed the music, but became embarrassed by how it made her face look when her cheeks puffed out to play. Because of this she threw it away and it landed on a riverbank where it was found by a <a href="/wiki/Satyr" title="Satyr">satyr</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Theoi_Classical_Texts_Library-2_34-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Theoi_Classical_Texts_Library-2-34"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Worship_in_Rome_and_Italy">Worship in Rome and Italy</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=12" title="Edit section: Worship in Rome and Italy"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Minerva_1.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Minerva_1.jpg/250px-Minerva_1.jpg" decoding="async" width="190" height="304" style="--mw-file-upright: 0.75" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Minerva_1.jpg/500px-Minerva_1.jpg 2x" data-file-width="616" data-file-height="986"></a><figcaption>Fresco of Minerva (helmeted figure on right) from the Villa San Marco, <a href="/wiki/Stabiae" title="Stabiae">Stabiae</a> (1st century AD)</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Athena-Schale_Hildesheimer_Silberfund.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Athena-Schale_Hildesheimer_Silberfund.jpg/250px-Athena-Schale_Hildesheimer_Silberfund.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="192" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Athena-Schale_Hildesheimer_Silberfund.jpg/500px-Athena-Schale_Hildesheimer_Silberfund.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3067" data-file-height="2358"></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Raised-relief" class="mw-redirect" title="Raised-relief">Raised-relief</a> image of Minerva on a Roman gilt silver bowl, first century BC</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Sbeitla_06.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Sbeitla_06.jpg/250px-Sbeitla_06.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="187" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Sbeitla_06.jpg/500px-Sbeitla_06.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2288" data-file-height="1712"></a><figcaption>Temple of Minerva in <a href="/wiki/Sbeitla" title="Sbeitla">Sbeitla</a>, Tunisia</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Bronze_Head_of_Sulis_Minerva.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Bronze_Head_of_Sulis_Minerva.jpg/250px-Bronze_Head_of_Sulis_Minerva.jpg" decoding="async" width="190" height="253" style="--mw-file-upright: 0.75" class="mw-file-element mw-file-upright" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Bronze_Head_of_Sulis_Minerva.jpg/500px-Bronze_Head_of_Sulis_Minerva.jpg 2x" data-file-width="799" data-file-height="1065"></a><figcaption>A head of "Sulis-Minerva" found in the ruins of the <a href="/wiki/Roman_Baths_(Bath)" title="Roman Baths (Bath)">Roman baths in Bath</a></figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Domitian_Denarius_Minerva_RIC_167_1.xcf" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Silver denarius of the Roman Emperor Domitian dated c. 90 AD" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Domitian_Denarius_Minerva_RIC_167_1.xcf/250px-Domitian_Denarius_Minerva_RIC_167_1.xcf.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="116" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Domitian_Denarius_Minerva_RIC_167_1.xcf/500px-Domitian_Denarius_Minerva_RIC_167_1.xcf.png 2x" data-file-width="2382" data-file-height="1101"></a><figcaption>Silver denarius of the Roman Emperor Domitianus (Domitian) featuring Minerva, dated <abbr title="circa">c.</abbr><span style="white-space:nowrap;"> 90 AD</span>, IMP CAES DOMIT AVG GERM P M TR P VIIII, laureate head right; IMP XXI COS XV CENS P P P, Minerva standing left, holding spear and thunderbolt, shield resting against back of leg; References: BMC 167, RIC 691, RSC 260, Paris 159, Cohen 260</figcaption></figure>
<p>The Romans celebrated her festival from March 19 to March 23 during the day that is called, in the neuter plural, Quinquatria, the fifth day after the Ides of March, the nineteenth, an <a href="/wiki/Artisan" title="Artisan">artisans</a>' holiday. This festival was of deepest importance to artists and craftsmen as she was the patron goddess of crafting and arts.<sup id="cite_ref-35" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-35"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> According to <a href="/wiki/Ovid" title="Ovid">Ovid</a> (<a href="/wiki/Fasti_(poem)" title="Fasti (poem)">Fasti</a> 3.809) the festival was 5 days long, and the first day was said to be the anniversary of Minerva's birth, so no blood was to be shed. The following four days were full of games of "drawn swords" in honour of Minerva's military association.<sup id="cite_ref-Theoi_Classical_Texts_Library_36-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Theoi_Classical_Texts_Library-36"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Suetonius tells us (Life of Domitian 4.4) that Domitian celebrated the Quinquatria by appointing a college of priests who were to stage plays and animal games in addition to poetry and oratory competitions.<sup id="cite_ref-37" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-37"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> A lesser version, the <i>Minusculae Quinquatria</i>, was held on the Ides of June, June 13, by the <a href="/wiki/Auloi" class="mw-redirect" title="Auloi">flute-players</a>, as Minerva was thought to have invented the flute.<sup id="cite_ref-Theoi_Classical_Texts_Library-2_34-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Theoi_Classical_Texts_Library-2-34"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> In 207 BC, a <a href="/wiki/Roman_guild" class="mw-redirect" title="Roman guild">guild</a> of poets and actors was formed to meet and make <a href="/wiki/Votive_offering" title="Votive offering">votive offerings</a> at the temple of Minerva on the <a href="/wiki/Aventine_Hill" title="Aventine Hill">Aventine Hill</a>. Among others, its members included <a href="/wiki/Livius_Andronicus" title="Livius Andronicus">Livius Andronicus</a>. The Aventine sanctuary of Minerva continued to be an important center of the arts for much of the middle <a href="/wiki/Roman_Republic" title="Roman Republic">Roman Republic</a>.
</p><p>As <i>Minerva Medica</i>, she was the goddess of medicine and physicians. As <i>Minerva Achaea</i>, she was worshipped at <a href="/wiki/Lucera" title="Lucera">Lucera</a> in <a href="/wiki/Apulia" title="Apulia">Apulia</a> where <a href="/wiki/Votive_gift" class="mw-redirect" title="Votive gift">votive gifts</a> and arms said to be those of <a href="/wiki/Diomedes" title="Diomedes">Diomedes</a> were preserved in her temple.<sup id="cite_ref-38" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-38"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-dgrbm2_39-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dgrbm2-39"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>According to the <a href="/wiki/Acta_Arvalia" title="Acta Arvalia">Acta Arvalia</a>, a cow was sacrificed to Minerva on October 13 58 AD along with many other sacrifices to celebrate the anniversary of Nero coming to power. On January 3 81 AD, as a part of the New Year vows, two cows were sacrificed to Minerva (among many others) to secure the well-being of the emperor Titus, Domitian Caesar, Julia Augusta, and their children. On January 3 87 AD there is again record of a cow being sacrificed to Minerva among the many sacrifices made as a part of the New Year vows.<sup id="cite_ref-40" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-40"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In <i><a href="/wiki/Fasti_(poem)" title="Fasti (poem)">Fasti</a></i> III, <a href="/wiki/Ovid" title="Ovid">Ovid</a> called her the "goddess of a thousand works"<sup id="cite_ref-Theoi_Classical_Texts_Library_36-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Theoi_Classical_Texts_Library-36"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> due to all of the things she was associated with. Minerva was worshipped throughout Italy, and when she eventually became equated with the Greek goddess Athena, she also became a goddess of battle. Unlike Mars, god of war, she was sometimes portrayed with sword lowered, in sympathy for the recent dead, rather than raised in triumph and battle lust. In Rome her bellicose nature was emphasized less than elsewhere.<sup id="cite_ref-41" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-41"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>According to <a href="/wiki/Livy" title="Livy">Livy</a>'s <i>History of Rome</i> (7.3), the annual nail marking the year, a process where the praetor maximus drove a nail in to formally keep track of the current year, happened in the temple of Minerva because she was thought to have invented numbers.<sup id="cite_ref-42" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-42"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>42<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup><sup id="cite_ref-MacRae,_Duncan_2016_43-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MacRae,_Duncan_2016-43"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>There is archaeological evidence to suggest that Minerva was worshipped not only in a formal civic fashion, but also by individuals on a more personal level.<sup id="cite_ref-MacRae,_Duncan_2016_43-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MacRae,_Duncan_2016-43"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Roman_coinage">Roman coinage</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=13" title="Edit section: Roman coinage"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>Minerva is featured on the coinage of different <a href="/wiki/Roman_emperor" title="Roman emperor">Roman emperors</a>. She often is represented on the reverse side of a coin holding an owl and a spear among her attributes.<sup id="cite_ref-44" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-44"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Worship_in_Roman_Britain">Worship in Roman Britain</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=14" title="Edit section: Worship in Roman Britain"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>During <a href="/wiki/Roman_Britain" title="Roman Britain">the Roman occupation of Britain</a>, it was common for carpenters to own tools ornamented with images of Minerva to invoke a greater amount of protection from the goddess of crafts. Some women would also have images of her on accessories such as hairpins or jewellery. She was even featured on some funerary art on coffins and signet rings.<sup id="cite_ref-Henig-1984_45-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Henig-1984-45"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Bath">Bath</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=15" title="Edit section: Bath"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>During Roman rule, Minerva became equated with the Celtic goddess <a href="/wiki/Sulis" title="Sulis">Sulis</a>, to the degree where their names were used both together and interchangeably.<sup id="cite_ref-Henig-1984_45-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Henig-1984-45"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> She was believed to preside over the healing hot springs located in <a href="/wiki/Bath,_Somerset" title="Bath, Somerset">Bath</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Sauer-1996_46-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sauer-1996-46"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup> Though Minerva is not a water deity, her association with intellectual professions as <i>Minerva Medica</i> she could also be thought of as a healing goddess, the epigraphic evidence present makes it clear that this is how Minerva was thought of in Bath.<sup id="cite_ref-Sauer-1996_46-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sauer-1996-46"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Some of the archaeological evidence present in Bath leads scholars to believe that it was thought Minerva could provide full healing from things such as rheumatism via the hot springs if she was given full credit for the healing.<sup id="cite_ref-Henig-1984_45-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Henig-1984-45"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The temple of Sulis Minerva was known for having a miraculous altar-fire that burned coal as opposed to the traditional wood.<sup id="cite_ref-Henig-1984_45-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Henig-1984-45"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Carrawburgh">Carrawburgh</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=16" title="Edit section: Carrawburgh"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>There is evidence of worship of Minerva Medica in <a href="/wiki/Carrawburgh" title="Carrawburgh">Carrawburgh</a> due to archaeological evidence such as a relief depicting her and <a href="/wiki/Asclepius" title="Asclepius">Aesculapius</a>.<sup id="cite_ref-Sauer-1996_46-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sauer-1996-46"><span class="cite-bracket">[</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">]</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Chester">Chester</h3><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=17" title="Edit section: Chester"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<p>There is a shrine dedicated to Minerva in Edgar's Field built in the face of a quarry next to the <a href="/wiki/River_Dee,_Wales" title="River Dee, Wales">River Dee</a>.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Etruscan_Menrva">Etruscan Menrva</h2><span class="mw-editsection"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a href="/w/index.php?title=Minerva&action=edit&section=18" title="Edit section: Etruscan Menrva"><span>edit</span></a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320"><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Menrva" title="Menrva">Menrva</a></div>
<p>Stemming from an Italic goddess <span title="uncoded-language text">*<i lang="mis">Meneswā</i></span>, the <a href="/wiki/Etruscan_religion" title="Etruscan religion">Etruscans</a> adopted the inherited Old Latin name, <span title="Latin-language text">*<i lang="la">Menerwā</i></span>, thereby calling her <a href="/wiki/Menrva" title="Menrva">Menrva</a>. It is presumed that her Roman name, Minerva, is based on this <a href="/wiki/Etruscan_mythology" class="mw-redirect" title="Etruscan mythology">Etruscan mythology</a>. Minerva was the goddess of wisdom, war, art, schools, justice and commerce. She was the Etruscan counterpart to Greek <a href="/wiki/Athena" title="Athena">Athena</a>. Like Athena, Minerva burst from the head of her father, Jupiter (Greek <a href="/wiki/Zeus" title="Zeus">Zeus</a>), who had devoured her mother (Metis) in an unsuccessful attempt to prevent her birth.
</p><p>By a process of <a href="/wiki/Folk_etymology" title="Folk etymology">folk etymology</a>, the Romans could have linked her foreign name to the <a href="/wiki/Root_(linguistics)" title="Root (linguistics)">root</a> <i>men-</i> in <a href="/wiki/Latin_language" class="mw-redirect" title="Latin language">Latin</a> <a href="/wiki/Word" title="Word">words</a> such as <i>mens</i> meaning "mind", perhaps because one of her aspects as goddess pertained to the intellectual. The word <i>mens</i> is built from the <a href="/wiki/PIE" class="mw-redirect" title="PIE">Proto-Indo-European</a> root <i>*men-</i> 'mind' (linked with memory as in Greek <a href="/wiki/Mnemosyne" title="Mnemosyne">Mnemosyne</a> (<i><span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language text"><span lang="grc">μνημοσύνη</span></span></i>, <span title="Ancient Greek transliteration" lang="grc-Latn"><i>mnēmosúnē</i></span>) and <span title="Ancient Greek transliteration" lang="grc-Latn"><i>mnêstis</i></span> (<i><span title="Ancient Greek (to 1453)-language text"><span lang="grc">μνῆστις</span></span></i>): memory, remembrance, recollection, <span title="Sanskrit-language romanization"><i lang="sa-Latn">manush</i></span> in Sanskrit meaning mind).<sup class="noprint Inline-Template Template-Fact" style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed" title="Wikipedia:Citation needed"><span title="This claim needs references to reliable sources. (November 2025)">citation needed</span></a></i>]</sup>
</p><p>The Etruscan Menrva was part of a holy <a href="/wiki/Triad_(relationship)" class="mw-redirect" title="Triad (relationship)">triad</a> with <a href="/wiki/Tinia" title="Tinia">Tinia</a> and <a href="/wiki/Uni_(mythology)" title="Uni (mythology)">Uni</a>, equivalent to the Roman <a href="/wiki/Capitoline_Triad" title="Capitoline Triad">Capitoline Triad</a> of Jupiter-Juno-Minerva.
</p>
